era_context: Antoninus Pius's reign continues in the Roman Empire, now in its 15th year. The Han Dynasty in China is in advanced decline, with Emperor Xian on the throne. The Parthian Empire remains stable under Vologases IV. In India, the Satavahana Empire is in its final century. This period represents the apex of the Pax Romana and the beginning of the end for the Han Dynasty—a moment of apparent stability masking underlying structural crises.

events.title: Roman consulate of Sextus Erucius Clarus and Gaius Sextius Lateranus
events.description: The consulate of 153 CE saw Sextus Erucius Clarus and Gaius Sextius Lateranus as consuls. This was a routine administrative year in the Roman Empire under Antoninus Pius. The consulate itself was a ceremonial and administrative position, and no major events are recorded for these consuls. The year is documented in the Fasti Consulares (official list of consuls), which provides a framework for dating other events but little substantive historical information about 153 itself.
events.certainty_note: The consulate is confirmed by the Fasti Consulares, which are contemporary epigraphic records. However, no substantive events are recorded for this year.

events.title: Han Dynasty continues administrative fragmentation; no major recorded events
events.description: The year 153 CE falls within the reign of Emperor Xian of Han (r. 146–168), a period of continued administrative decline. No specific events are recorded for 153 in surviving Chinese sources. The broader pattern of eunuch dominance, scholar-official resistance, and regional military fragmentation continued. The Han Dynasty was approaching its final collapse, which would occur in 220 CE. This year represents the continuation of a long-term structural crisis rather than a moment of acute crisis.

sources.name: Sanguozhi (Records of the Three Kingdoms) by Chen Shou

events.certainty_note: No specific events are recorded for 153 in surviving sources. The characterization is based on the broader pattern of Han decline during Emperor Xian's reign.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The sparsity of recorded events reflects the limitations of surviving sources rather than any disputed historical claims.
historiographic_note: The year 153 CE is extremely poorly documented. No major events are recorded in surviving sources for either the Roman Empire or China. The historical record for this year consists almost entirely of administrative records (consular lists) and the absence of recorded crises. This reflects both the stability of the period and the selective nature of historical documentation—routine administrative years are rarely recorded in detail. Sources are heavily weighted toward the Roman Empire and China; other regions are entirely absent from the historical record.

graph_edges.note: Part of the same period of Roman imperial stability under Antoninus Pius
